words: Edward Swenson came to the Park valley with his parents in 1891
at the age of eight. They were one of the first families to settle in
this little Norwegian community, a remote clearing surrounded by
wilderness until Potlatch logging came in the teens, Ed describes
their first trip from Moscow to Park, building their homestead cabin
and the first survey, the appearance of the valley and its first two
settlers, experiences with neighbors and Indians, and the tribulations
of building a road which would connect Park with the world outside.
SAM SCHRAGER: Do you know why your parents decided to leave Norway and to come
to here?
EDWARD SUENSON: Yes, My father's older brother came before they did and found
land in Minnesota, you know. And to the people back in Norway, a hundred and
sixty acres of land would be a big farm. And they had heard they could get
land on a homestead here, so they decided to go. They saved up enough money
for the voyage, you know, and came, and his brother met them in New York,
and they came on through. They lived three years in Uisconsin to get a start
for homesteading, you know, and then moved into Minnesota and located there
with his brother as a neighbor. So they worked together in building up, you
know, and got started there. They lived there about 19 years, I believe it
was, before they came to Idaho,

speaker: SAM
words: Did you ever hear about what the conditions were like in Norway? Uas it

speaker: SAM
words: pretty hard in Norway?

speaker: ES
words: Uell, so for as I learned from the folks, it was not bad at all at that

speaker: ES
words: time. But of course for a young man to get started there wasn't so easy, be

speaker: ES
words: cause the land was all taken up pretty well, you know, and father wanted to

speaker: ES
words: be a farmer—that was what hefd learned there. So that's how they came then.

speaker: ES
words: And the ship that they came on was a sailship, of course, and it was the maiden

speaker: ES
words: voyage for that ship—it had never been on the ocean before. Uhen they got

speaker: ES
words: pretty well out on the ocean, why they run into a storm, and for three days

speaker: ES
words: they had to tackle back and forth against it to keep from going back, you

speaker: ES
words: know. And it was pretty rough. Father got seasick, but not so bad but he

speaker: ES
words: got over it. And mother had the two children, you know, to take care of.

speaker: ES
words: She said it didn't bother her so much,

speaker: SAM
words: So then they went to Uisconsin for a few years, and then Minnesota for

speaker: SAM
words: quite a while, but then they decided to leave Minnesota, Why did they decide

speaker: SAM
words: to come to Idaho?

speaker: ES
words: Well, the storms were so bad there. They lost two crops of grain two years

speaker: ES
words: in succession and that discouraged them pretty much. They had heard about

speaker: ES
words: this Idaho country, you know, and they decided they wanted to come Uest. My

speaker: ES
words: oldest brother went first. He went in the fall of 1890, And then we came

speaker: ES
words: in the spring of '91, and settled there together, and built up again. So

speaker: ES
words: the folks had quite an experience in pioneering,

speaker: SAM
words: They did it more than once,

speaker: ES
words: Well, you see they came out there on the prairie in Minnesota, no town

speaker: ES
words: nearby, and they had to build up the best they could right in the wilderness.

speaker: ES
words: There had been an Indian war the year before they came there, but that was

speaker: ES
words: settled, so they were safe in that respect. And then no neighbors around

speaker: ES
words: close by, you know. And they started with oxen and wagon to get to town, and

speaker: ES
words: that took a long time, you know. Mother had to be alone there with the little

speaker: ES
words: children until he'd get back. So it wasn't so good,

speaker: SAM
words: How did they decide to come out here? Uhat means did they use to get out?

speaker: ES
words: We came on an immigrant train from I think it was Uilmer, Minnesota, to

speaker: ES
words: Troy, Idaho, But in the meantime there had been a slide of land onto the

speaker: ES
words: railroad track between Troy and Moscow, so we had to get off at Moscow and go

speaker: ES
words: by wagon from there on.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you remember what that trip was like by wagon out to the Park country?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, My oldest brother met us there, you know, with team and wagon, and

speaker: ES
words: we'd go part ways. It took us three days from Moscow to Park, Idaho. Very

speaker: ES
words: bad roads, you know. My brother and I walked much of the time and father

speaker: ES
words: walked most of the time. So what little baggage we had was on the wagon and

speaker: ES
words: my mother and my sister would ride in the wagon. There was a farmstead I'd

speaker: ES
words: say about a mile east from Joel down there. There was a station there, rail

speaker: ES
words: road station, stayed the first night. The second night we got out to Texas

speaker: ES
words: Ridge and stayed there. And it took us about a day from there to get over to

speaker: ES
words: Park. Uhen we got down to the river the horses had to ford the river. There

speaker: ES
words: was no bridge you know, and the mater was high, and they had a foot log there

speaker: ES
words: for us to walk across. And when they got across the river they had to take

speaker: ES
words: the wagon apart and use just the hind part of the wagon to carry what few

speaker: ES
words: things they could load on there, to get up the hill on the east side. And

speaker: ES
words: then the next day they came back to get the rest of the things. There wasn't

speaker: ES
words: much road there. So we walked all the way across the canyon till we got over

speaker: ES
words: to Park. It was just about sunset when we came to my brother's place there,

speaker: SAM
words: Uhere did you cross the canyon?

speaker: ES
words: There was a farmstead named Buckaloo on the west banks of the canyon, and

speaker: ES
words: there was a road down from their place, so some of our things were left there.

speaker: ES
words: And there was a kind of a makeshift road~it wasn't graded much, so they had

speaker: ES
words: to lock the hind wheels, roughlock the hind wheels on the wagon to hold it

speaker: ES
words: back, you know. And they had chains along. They cut down a little fir tree

speaker: ES
words: and tied behind the wagon to help hold it back. And made it down the hill,

speaker: ES
words: I can remember that because I thought it was so odd to see that tree dragging

speaker: ES
words: behind the wagon, you know (chuckles). Yeah,

speaker: SAM
words: About where would that be today?

speaker: ES
words: From Deary it'd be just about four miles south and then east, Ue didn't

speaker: ES
words: go by Deary at those times, you know. There was a road further south we got

speaker: ES
words: across on,

speaker: SAM
words: well why did they go so far? Uhy did your father decide to go all the way

speaker: SAM
words: to Park?

speaker: ES
words: Because my oldest brother was there and two of his wife's brothers were there

speaker: ES
words: at

speaker: ES
words: Park, And they had farmed, they had land connected together, you know. And

speaker: ES
words: of course we wanted to be with them. So they went across there. And it was

speaker: ES
words: so nice—the valley laid there so nice, timber scattered over, and not much

speaker: ES
words: underbrush, you know. And it looked so nice that they wanted to be there.

speaker: ES
words: And that was why later on when they formed a post office there they named it

speaker: ES
words: Park.

speaker: SAM
words: Could you expand on what that country looked like when you first saw it,

speaker: SAM
words: what it looked like to you?

speaker: ES
words: Uell from the first, the beginning, why there was nothing, there wasn't even

speaker: ES
words: any fences or anything, you know. The land wasn't surveyed yet. Each settled

speaker: ES
words: where he thought there might be a dividing line. And then eventually they

speaker: ES
words: got a survey ran across from a section line on Texas Ridge straight through

speaker: ES
words: the valley, and that was staked out. And then the farmers would measure out

speaker: ES
words: from that section line, north and south, and get pretty well located, until

speaker: ES
words: it was surveyed permanently, I can remember father was with the surveyor,

speaker: ES
words: and when they came to a section corner—they were running a line east and

speaker: ES
words: west—when they came to a section corner, that was not far from where father

speaker: ES
words: had built the house. So father asked the surveyor to view out the line north

speaker: ES
words: and south through what would be his west line. And there had been timber burnt

speaker: ES
words: down, and there was a big pine stump by itself, and that happened to be right

speaker: ES
words: on the line, on the west line of our place. So the surveyor told him that

speaker: ES
words: will be your landmark for your west line. Then he measured up from the section

speaker: ES
words: corner to get the south line. Then he started from there. But some of the

speaker: ES
words: farmers there would be kinda of off. They would be too close to the neighbor

speaker: ES
words: when they finally got their lines run, so they would move back then into a

speaker: ES
words: better location. My father had built the log house, and he had the logs

speaker: ES
words: built up pretty well the height they wanted. And when they measured up, why

speaker: ES
words: we were oh about five or ten yards in on our neighbor's place. So they had

speaker: ES
words: to tear the logs down and move them. But that didn't take long because they

speaker: ES
words: marked each log for each side, you know, and as they moved them up to the

speaker: ES
words: permanent side, the logs would be laid on the side where they had been before,

speaker: ES
words: and just rolled 'em back up again,

speaker: SAM
words: They didn't have the roof on the place by the time he realized he had to

speaker: SAM
words: move it?

speaker: ES
words: The roof wasn't on yet when they had to move it, so it didn't take long to

speaker: ES
words: move the walls, you know. And the foundation was just a log. They'd smooth

speaker: ES
words: the ground out and started with a log on the bottom and up, and then they

speaker: ES
words: knocked in the floor just about a log high off'n the ground. So the floor

speaker: ES
words: would be up from the ground aways. I can remember when they built that. I

speaker: ES
words: wasn't able to do any work but I was always with father, you know, when he was

speaker: ES
words: working there. Mother was staying with my oldest brother, mother and my

speaker: ES
words: sister. But wherever the men went I was right with them there.

speaker: SAM
words: Did it take 'em very long to build?

speaker: ES
words: No. Several neighbors get together, you know, and it wouldn't take long,

speaker: ES
words: and they knew how to fit the logs together, and it didn't take long. They

speaker: ES
words: cut down green trees and peeled the logs and notched them together. And they

speaker: ES
words: found cedar there, cedar was the best to split for shakes, you know, for the

speaker: ES
words: roof. And so they split shakes and put on for the roof. It didn't cost very

speaker: ES
words: much to build those days, except a lot of hard work.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you tell me that your father found a man that really knew how to use

speaker: SAM
words: the broad ax to do,,?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, yes, that was a neighbor. He had come in there just after we did. So

speaker: ES
words: we lived in the old cabin you know for I think about eight years. So father

speaker: ES
words: made a deal with this man to hew the logs for what we called the new house,

speaker: ES
words: you know.

speaker: SAM
words: Oh I see.

speaker: ES
words: And there was no money exchanged. This man needed a lot of fence rails to

speaker: ES
words: fence his land in. And we had cedar there to make rails from, and so father

speaker: ES
words: made a deal with him to pay him with fence rails. The nan wanted $20 to hew

speaker: ES
words: the logs for the house. And fence rails was valued at a cent each then,

speaker: ES
words: twelve feet long. So father made 2,000 rails in payment for hewing the logs

speaker: ES
words: (chuckles). And by that time why we had the horses and wagon, and so we

speaker: ES
words: delivered the fence rails over to the man's place. And he stayed with us, he

speaker: ES
words: had room and board with us while he hewed the logs.

speaker: SAM
words: Uell getting back to the first—the old cabin—that you built, was that the

speaker: SAM
words: first thing that your family did when they got there, was build?

speaker: ES
words: Oh yes, yeah, he located a spring of water, and not very far from the house,

speaker: ES
words: and then he got ready to build. You see we came in the spring of the year,

speaker: ES
words: and before fall, why we were in our own home there. Then he built a barn

speaker: ES
words: and chicken house and things like that, so. He had to stay home and work all

speaker: ES
words: that summer. Then the next year when harvest time came, why he went out and

speaker: ES
words: worked in the harvest fields. Through the harvest and then back, and they

speaker: ES
words: would work on the farm through winter, falling trees and clearing land, you

speaker: ES
words: know, to get broke up next summer. So there wasn't much idle time in those

speaker: ES
words: days.

speaker: SAM
words: Uhere did he go to work in the harvest?

speaker: ES
words: Out in what's called the Genesee l/alley. It's farmland, lived where they

speaker: ES
words: were about seven miles north and east from Genesee, where he worked. They

speaker: ES
words: raised mostly wheat there, and they used binders to cut it with, and then they

speaker: ES
words: stacked it. Then when all the district was stacked, then they'd start thrashing.

speaker: ES
words: So he worked until they got through stacking. He was good at stacking grain.

speaker: ES
words: And he worked through till that.

speaker: SAM
words: So he'd just go out for the harvest season and then he came back right away?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, he'd come back then. They'd put up hay. The wild grass grew tall there.

speaker: ES
words: There was no cattle much around, you know, then, just a cow or two for milk.

speaker: ES
words: There was not any young cattle running there then. So the grass grew up good.

speaker: ES
words: And he was pretty good with a hand scythe, and he'd go out and cut the grass,

speaker: ES
words: and we boys raked it up into little bunches, you know, shocked it up and then

speaker: ES
words: hauled it home. There was so much open land so you'd go most anywhere and

speaker: ES
words: cut the hay, you know. There wasn't much brush or other small trees. It was

speaker: ES
words: really nice there. You'd cut grass along those big pine trees, you know.

speaker: ES
words: had only one cow the first winter. Then the next year we had more. But it

speaker: ES
words: took a few years before he got enough ground broke up, you know, to raise any

speaker: ES
words: hay. So we had that grass. And I remember one winter we were short of hay

speaker: ES
words: for the cows, and the cows like the moss that grew on the pine trees. And

speaker: ES
words: they were cutting down trees to clear the land anyway, so the cows would come

speaker: ES
words: and eat the moss off of the branches, and then he fed 'em hay at night. So

speaker: ES
words: we made it through.

speaker: SAM
words: This land being so clear of brush, is that because the Indians kept it burnt?

speaker: ES
words: Uell there were stories about that, that the Indians would set fires when

speaker: ES
words: they were coming back out of the woods. But there were also fires from

speaker: ES
words: lightning, thunder storms, which we knew happened after we came there,

speaker: ES
words: could see trees on fire once in awhile after rainstorm. And of course that

speaker: ES
words: would set the grass on fire. Ue kind of believed that that was pretty much

speaker: ES
words: what did that. It was claimed that the Indians didn't want the brush to grow

speaker: ES
words: because it was easier for them to ride through, but they formed trails, you

speaker: ES
words: know, when they went back into the mountains. They had one trail up from

speaker: ES
words: Kendrick and up through, and that trail came through our land, almost through

speaker: ES
words: the center of it, right close to where we had built our house. They came

speaker: ES
words: through there, and going back into the woods to hunt and fish and pick berries.

speaker: ES
words: And they had a camping place right close to where father built the barn. And

speaker: ES
words: one day they camped there. They staked their horses, Ue had broke up a

speaker: ES
words: few acres then, and had seeded to oats for hay. But they stayed on the same

speaker: ES
words: camping place, and staked their horses right in the oats field. The oats were

speaker: ES
words: out maybe five or six inches high. And they settled down to eat there.

speaker: ES
words: Father and the older boys were away that day, so it was mother and my sister

speaker: ES
words: and I. And so mother told me, she said, "You better go and tell them to move

speaker: ES
words: their horses off of the oats." Uell, I had heard the Indians were wild, you

speaker: ES
words: know, and I was pretty well scared of it, but I managed to get over there and

speaker: ES
words: showed 'em where they should stake their horses, you know. And they did,

speaker: ES
words: they started to move, put their horses over. I ran back to the house. Then

speaker: ES
words: mother told me she said, "They were nice to you," so she said, "ask them to

speaker: ES
words: come in and have their meal." well of course I couldn't talk to them, but

speaker: ES
words: somehow or other I got the message across. So they came to the house.

speaker: ES
words: There was five or six of 'em. And mother set the table out so they could eat

speaker: ES
words: at the table, but they put the table back where it was, and they set down on

speaker: ES
words: the floor in a circle and had their dinner. And mother had coffee for them,

speaker: ES
words: and sugar and cream and things like that, you know, a little fruit. So they

speaker: ES
words: really enjoyed it, and they managed to try to talk, you know, to each other.

speaker: ES
words: So then they went away, and then in the fall, when they came back, they stopped

speaker: ES
words: to buy deer hides. You know there was no season on deer, so when you were

speaker: ES
words: short of meat you'd go out and shoot a deer. And my older brother, he had

speaker: ES
words: a gun that he could shoot deer with, and so we always had deer meat when we

speaker: ES
words: wanted it. And we'd heard about this, that the Indians would buy the hides,

speaker: ES
words: so we had deer hides on hand. They would pay 500 for a deer hide. But mother

speaker: ES
words: thought that wasn't enough money so she held out for 750, And they, through

speaker: ES
words: motions and that, why they began to talk trade, you know, and she finally

speaker: ES
words: got the 750 (chuckles),

speaker: SAM
words: Did they pay in money or did they pay in trade?

speaker: ES
words: Oh yeah, they had money with 'em, I don't know—you know they used to

speaker: ES
words: make up moccasins and things like that and sell to the white people. So

speaker: ES
words: they'd have a little money. (Pause.) 'Course then afterwards we fenced

speaker: ES
words: the land in so they had to follow the roads then back in,

speaker: SAM
words: How many families would you say were there when you got there?

speaker: ES
words: I think there were about six families in there that next summer after we

speaker: ES
words: came, I couldn't say just what there was when we came, 'cause I don't remember

speaker: ES
words: that. But as they came in they began to get acquainted, you know, and visited

speaker: ES
words: back and forth. I can remember there was about six right in the main part of

speaker: ES
words: the valley there. And they began to help each other, you know. As each new

speaker: ES
words: settler came, why the others would go and help them get started, and neighbor

speaker: ES
words: with them, Ue didn't have any schoolhouse the first year we were there. The

speaker: ES
words: second year—that would be in 1892—they built the schoolhouse. By going

speaker: ES
words: together and getting logs, built up a log schoolhouse. So that fall we had

speaker: ES
words: a little school, but it wasn't until next spring that we really had a school

speaker: ES
words: term. But I can remember going to school after snow came in the fall. But

speaker: ES
words: then they waited till next spring then. So I was between nine and ten years

speaker: ES
words: old before I went to school. And I couldn't speak English (chuckles), but the

speaker: ES
words: school was in English, of course, Ue lived in a Norwegian settlement back

speaker: ES
words: East you know, back in Minnesota. But it didn't take long. Ue had a very

speaker: ES
words: good teacher, I can remember the first teacher we had, teacher named Ramsey.

speaker: ES
words: And he was very good with us children. He seemed like he was like one of us,

speaker: ES
words: you know. So we got along pretty good,

speaker: SAM
words: Uere the other families Norwegian too when you first got here?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, I know four of them were. There was one family, they were supposed

speaker: ES
words: to be French Canadians, Their language was a little different, I mean their

speaker: ES
words: English language was mixed a little with French words, you know. But they

speaker: ES
words: spoke pretty plain English anyway. But they didn't have children old enough

speaker: ES
words: to go to school until after they'd lived there awhile. Their first child

speaker: ES
words: was born after they settled there, so they were among the smaller ones when

speaker: ES
words: I quit school there, I went to school until I was about fourteen years old.

speaker: ES
words: By that time the older boys were out on their own pretty much, you know, and

speaker: ES
words: I had to help father with the work, and so I had to quit school, Ue had our

speaker: ES
words: school terms in the spring then, you know, and summer, Ue had only four

speaker: ES
words: months of school at first. So it didn't take long, but we managed.

speaker: SAM
words: Uas this teacher hired from the outside?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, and they boarded with some of the settlers in there then, you know,

speaker: ES
words: I can remember towards the end of the years that I went to school we had—

speaker: ES
words: I can't remember, I think her name was Longfellow, And she boarded with a

speaker: ES
words: Norwegian family. She couldn't speak Norwegian, but they managed that. And

speaker: ES
words: she had to apy $45 a month for board and room, and she was getting $60 a month

speaker: ES
words: for teaching,..

speaker: SAM
words: So she didn't have much left by the end of the term, huh?

speaker: ES
words: No, but you know a dollar was pretty big at those times, and wages were

speaker: ES
words: small.

speaker: SAM
words: Uhat about this fella that you mentioned to me before that you called "the

speaker: SAM
words: land grabber"? This guy sounds like a real character in the early days.

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, they had located there before we came. It was on his land that father

speaker: ES
words: first started his cabin. And when the survey was made that section line came

speaker: ES
words: right by his house, just a few rods from the house. So he was lucky to be

speaker: ES
words: built so he didn't have to move. And so we had to move again. And the reason

speaker: ES
words: why he was kind of a loner was, when he moved in there, he had a little money,

speaker: ES
words: and he brought a car load of cattle and furniture and stuff you know, horses

speaker: ES
words: and wagons. And so he was considered a little richer than the rest of em.

speaker: ES
words: That didn't go very good because the others were good neighbors, exchanged

speaker: ES
words: work and all, but he didn't. So it kinda created a friction there, you know.

speaker: ES
words: You know in those times, why law enforcement wasn't as strict as it was

speaker: ES
words: later on. A man could carry a gun any time he wanted to, and if he got crowded

speaker: ES
words: he might be tempted to use it. So he began to get a little bit afraid of

speaker: ES
words: some of his neighbors there, so he strapped a gun on his hip and carried that

speaker: ES
words: around with him (chuckles). And of course that didn't help any to be a

speaker: ES
words: neighbor. But eventually why he began to see he didn't gain anything by it,

speaker: ES
words: so he put his gun away and became a neighbor.

speaker: SAM
words: You told me he got into a big argument about where the school should ought

speaker: SAM
words: to be built?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, they started building a school, and he thought it was too far from his

speaker: ES
words: place, and he began to object. So there was another Norwegian joining him

speaker: ES
words: on the west of his land, and so he offered to give an acre of land to put the

speaker: ES
words: school over there. And the majority ruled on that, well he fought that, and

speaker: ES
words: he finally pulled his gun, but he didn't (chuckles) have time to use it. One

speaker: ES
words: of the men took the gun away from him and emptied the shots in the gun and

speaker: ES
words: stuck it in his own pocket. So they went to work and moved the logs over to

speaker: ES
words: the other place and started building there. Uhen they went home in the evening

speaker: ES
words: he give the gun back to this fella. After that he didn't carry it when he

speaker: ES
words: came to work,(chuckles). Uell of course there was a little fistfight included

speaker: ES
words: with it before they settled. He didn't stand any show there so...

speaker: SAM
words: Uell did they call him "the land grabber," because he tried to grab the good

speaker: SAM
words: land?

speaker: ES
words: Yes. Before the survey was made, he had run a temporary fence around over

speaker: ES
words: 200 acres of land there, you know. So when the survey was made, why he had

speaker: ES
words: to move his fences. He had a boy—one of his, the oldest boy in that family

speaker: ES
words: was the same age as I was. Uell at first why I kind of shied away from him,

speaker: ES
words: but we become good friends. Ue were in the same class in school. So we had

speaker: ES
words: no trouble at all. And eventually they began to get more neighborly. This

speaker: ES
words: man's wife and my mother were good friends. They got to visiting each other,

speaker: ES
words: and were real good friends. So the rest of us, like my sister and their girls,

speaker: ES
words: were good friends, you know. Ue began to neighbor back and forth, and things

speaker: ES
words: were pretty good.

speaker: SAM
words: what part of Park did he stake out for his place? I take it he was close

speaker: SAM
words: to the first person in there, and had a chance to take the place that he wanted

speaker: SAM
words: for his own.

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, each one when he came in, he just walked around there until he found

speaker: ES
words: where he'd like to be, and that's where they located.

speaker: SAM
words: Uhere did this guy pick for his land?

speaker: ES
words: He was right in the center of the valley. He was one of the first ones.

speaker: ES
words: And he located right in the center there, pretty well. Uell there were others

speaker: ES
words: just about as close in, but I mean the first group, y'know, that came in there,

speaker: ES
words: would locate in the center, and he was one of them. There were two others

speaker: ES
words: I know that were right close in. The line came pretty close to the buildings

speaker: ES
words: of the first three settlers there, three or four, and he was one of 'em.

speaker: SAM
words: How would you describe the shape of the Park country, what it looks like and

speaker: SAM
words: how big it is?

speaker: ES
words: the biggest part of the valley laid quite level. There were couple

speaker: ES
words: little creeks of water running through during high water time, and pretty

speaker: ES
words: well into summer, but in later years they dried up. So that was why too that

speaker: ES
words: they settled like that—they all wanted to be close to water, you know. And

speaker: ES
words: these little streams run through the land where they settled.

speaker: SAM
words: And the timber was mostly yellow pine growing in the open, and with grass?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, on the flat there was mostly yellow pine. And there was fir thickets,

speaker: ES
words: young trees. The fir likes to grow where there's moisture, so wherever the

speaker: ES
words: land would hold moisture long, that's where the fir trees would get started

speaker: ES
words: growing. So there was thickets of fir tree and then yellow pine scattered.

speaker: ES
words: There wasn't so much trees there that what you could look down through the

speaker: ES
words: valley, you know, all over there. That's why it looked just like a park,

speaker: ES
words: like the trees had been planted there.

speaker: SAM
words: And then around the Park area itself was forest, was woods?

speaker: ES
words: Oh yes. See the valley was surrounded on three sides by mountains, and the

speaker: ES
words: timber would grow down onto the flats. Uhenever the snow melted in the

speaker: ES
words: spring, a lot of that would seep down onto lower land you know, so there was

speaker: ES
words: moisture for the timber to grow. But out on the main valley, the main level

speaker: ES
words: land wouldn't be so much moisture, so the trees were more scattered.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you depend on wild game a lot in those days?

speaker: ES
words: Of course we did, because nobody had cattle to speak of. As they started

speaker: ES
words: settling there they would bring a milk cow or two, and it took awhile to have

speaker: ES
words: beef cattle. So it was venison, and they had grouse and partridge. And then

speaker: ES
words: there was some meadows east of there about five miles, and in high water in

speaker: ES
words: the spring there would be salmon come up there, so the men would go back there

speaker: ES
words: with their spears and bring home a few salmon. So we had a variety. And of

speaker: ES
words: course when they'd go out to town, if they had to have other meat they'd

speaker: ES
words: bring home some from there, but very seldom they would have to buy meat at

speaker: ES
words: the market. There was no season on game, you could kill as many as you wanted.

speaker: ES
words: And the first summer that my older brother lived out there, he traded venison

speaker: ES
words: for meat at the meat market in Troy. He'd go out and shoot some deer and

speaker: ES
words: dress 'em up and take em to town.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you say that the main part of your diet would be the venison and

speaker: SAM
words: other things that you shot in the wild in the first years, or would you have

speaker: SAM
words: a lot of store-bought food too?

speaker: ES
words: No, venison was the main meat supply. In the spring they'd have salmon,

speaker: ES
words: and then of course they had partridge and grouse for a change. There were

speaker: ES
words: bear scattered here and there. Before there were any dogs in the valley, why

speaker: ES
words: the bears would be roaming around, but it seemed like people at that time

speaker: ES
words: didn't take to bear meat much because they didn't eat it. But occassionally

speaker: ES
words: there was some that would kill a bear and use that meat.

speaker: SAM
words: Now for supplies in the early years when you were first there, where did you

speaker: SAM
words: go and how did you get there?

speaker: ES
words: Well we had Kendrick on the southwest from us and Troy on west side. There

speaker: ES
words: wasn't much road to either place. But the neighbors got together and talked

speaker: ES
words: over which would be the best route to take to town, 'cause they had to build

speaker: ES
words: a road, you know. So Troy was chosen as the best town. It was the biggest

speaker: ES
words: town then, Troy was built before Kendrick was. And so the farmers went

speaker: ES
words: together, and they chose a couple of men to represent the district to go to

speaker: ES
words: Troy to see what deal they could make with the merchants there, because they

speaker: ES
words: needed to have a supply of provisions, groceries, to have while working on

speaker: ES
words: the road. They couldn't go out and earn money any other way then, you know.

speaker: ES
words: It took a lot of time and work to build that road, especially across the

speaker: ES
words: Potlatch canyon. So the grocers in Troy agreed to furnish groceries and

speaker: ES
words: dynamite for blasting. And so the men could spend their time working on

speaker: ES
words: that. And by that time the settlement had grown so there were oh probably a

speaker: ES
words: dozen or more of good young men to work on the road. And they had horses

speaker: ES
words: and plows for plowing up wherever they could. But in the canyon they couldn't

speaker: ES
words: use much of that. They used the horses to kind of drag the trees from where

speaker: ES
words: they were building the road. They'd cut down trees and there were trees

speaker: ES
words: laying down, and they'd cut off enough of the tree to have a space to drive

speaker: ES
words: through, you know. And they'd use the horses to drag those logs out of the

speaker: ES
words: way. The rest of it was handwork. There was a bachelor there that had done

speaker: ES
words: some mining, and he was familiar with using dynamite, so when they run into

speaker: ES
words: rock, why he would do the blasting. So they went on.

speaker: SAM
words: Uell, where did the road go to connect up with the main road there?

speaker: ES
words: It was a little further up the river, further north. There were some

speaker: ES
words: homesteaders on the west side named Morris, two brothers and their mother and

speaker: ES
words: father. And they had a road out to their place, you know, out on the breaks

speaker: ES
words: of the canyon, so the people at Park then continued on from that road and down

speaker: ES
words: across the canyon. There was no bridge there neither the first few years, but

speaker: ES
words: the main thing was to get the road—they could ford the river. So after they

speaker: ES
words: had the road built, then they built a bridge.

speaker: SAM
words: Uas this road very switchbacky?

speaker: ES
words: Yes. On the west side was quite a flat. There was no what you might call

speaker: ES
words: creeks running down to cause any depression in the soil there, so they could

speaker: ES
words: go quite aways. They only made three switchbacks on the west side to get up.

speaker: ES
words: But on the east side there were fifteen switchbacks. They followed the ridge

speaker: ES
words: pretty well on the east side to go up. And those switchbacks, they were

speaker: ES
words: switchbacks OK. They were turned pretty short. Uhen you got to dig into a

speaker: ES
words: steep sidehill to make a turn, why you don't dig very wide. But they made

speaker: ES
words: it. I know you couldn't drive four horses around those switchbacks. So

speaker: ES
words: they managed to get along anyway.

speaker: SAM
words: It was hand tools and muscle power, huh?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, pick and shovel and ax and a crosscut saw—that was the equipment they

speaker: ES
words: had.

speaker: SAM
words: I imagine it took them a real long time to put it in too.

speaker: ES
words: I can't remember how long it took because I was only about nine, ten years

speaker: ES
words: old then, you know. So I don't remember that.

speaker: SAM
words: About where did it connect up with the road into Troy?

speaker: ES
words: Well, you know the land settled up, as a rule they'd start close to town

speaker: ES
words: to settle up you know, and as they settled back, why they built roads through.

speaker: ES
words: And that road was built clear out to the breaks of the Potlatch canyon. So

speaker: ES
words: then when we got connected up, why we followed their roads on to Troy.

speaker: SAM
words: Well before that time where did the people trade, before they put that

speaker: SAM
words: road in?

speaker: ES
words: Some went to Kendrick and some went to Troy. They had to go horseback and

speaker: ES
words: pack the goods in then, you know. And there was a post office located pretty

speaker: ES
words: well towards the first road they had in there, and they had a little grocery

speaker: ES
words: store there. So the men would go horseback across there and load their groceries

speaker: ES
words: on their saddle and pack it home that way. And get the mail there, until they

speaker: ES
words: got the other road built.

speaker: SAM
words: Was this Anderson?

speaker: ES
words: Anderson was the name. That was the name of the people that homesteaded

speaker: ES
words: there.

speaker: SAM
words: Was there an actual trail there or would it be just a crosscountry?

speaker: ES
words: Well I suppose they cut a kind of a trail through, you know, so they could

speaker: ES
words: go through with a pack horse. The first two men that ever came to that valley

speaker: ES
words: were two miners. Big husky men—one was named Durgesen and the other one was

speaker: ES
words: name Goldstrom. Ourgesen was a Norwegian and Goldstrom was a Swede. And

speaker: ES
words: they walked across the canyon, they were prospecting. And they walked across

speaker: ES
words: the canyon and came in on that valley, and it looked so nice in there they

speaker: ES
words: decided to settle there. So they staked out where they wanted to be, and

speaker: ES
words: then they went back to bring in supplies. They even carried their own cookstove

speaker: ES
words: in on their back, and other supplies that they needed, and built a cabin

speaker: ES
words: and settled down. They were pretty close to the edge of the canyon there where

speaker: ES
words: they settled.

speaker: SAM
words: Were they still there when your family came?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, they were there when we came. Of course the stoves that they carried

speaker: ES
words: across were not the full size cookstove, they were little bitty things that

speaker: ES
words: they could carry, but they would do, Uhen there was just one man in each

speaker: ES
words: house, you know, why they managed.

speaker: SAM
words: Would a guy have to lead his horse and pack the supplies on top of it?

speaker: ES
words: Well, not when we came. They had that first road where we came through with

speaker: ES
words: a wagon, you know. So they would use that road until they built the other

speaker: ES
words: one.

speaker: SAM
words: And that road went fairly close to Anderson?

speaker: ES
words: It was further north than Anderson, if I remember correctly. I'm not sure

speaker: ES
words: about this.

speaker: SAM
words: What about this other road? You told me that there was another one that

speaker: SAM
words: wound up getting built somehow in a crazy location.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, that was about 1908 or''09. There was a little friction there about

speaker: ES
words: that. Some of the neighbors there wanted a certain route close to their place,

speaker: ES
words: you know, and the others wanted another route. So we got a surveyor to come

speaker: ES
words: in, and he was more friendly to the people on what we call the east road,

speaker: ES
words: the east route. That went north across the mountain and over towards Helmer,

speaker: ES
words: after the Potlatch had built their road through. So they made a survey on

speaker: ES
words: that route, Uhen the surveyor got to the top of the mountain, he began to

speaker: ES
words: look around, and he saw there was no good place to go down without a lot of

speaker: ES
words: grading. So he stopped there, and he went back to Moscow and reported in

speaker: ES
words: on that. Then it wasn't long till there was another route started, and he

speaker: ES
words: went back and surveyed a part of that, and it would have been a much better

speaker: ES
words: route. By that time election came and he didn't get elected for the next term.

speaker: ES
words: They got another man voted in, and he went there, and he voted in favor of

speaker: ES
words: the east route. And that was built. But it just wasn't used very long,

speaker: ES
words: because by that time the Potlatch Company built a road into Park for logging,

speaker: ES
words: built a railroad in for logging out their timber. And as soon as that was

speaker: ES
words: finished, why when they pulled the rails out of there, why the settlers in

speaker: ES
words: there built that into a permanent road to market. And I believe that road is

speaker: ES
words: still being used.

speaker: SAM
words: Uell what you say sounds like it was just a very isolated piece of country

speaker: SAM
words: in the early days. Isolated from the...

speaker: ES
words: Between us and the market, yes. On that route that was mostly just timber.

speaker: ES
words: And I remember we had to get permission from the Potlatch Lumber Company to

speaker: ES
words: build that road, because they owned the timber through there, and they objected

speaker: ES
words: to having timber cut down just to build a road. So we made up a petition,

speaker: ES
words: and took over to Potlatch to get their signature, and I was appointed to take

speaker: ES
words: that over there. And Uilliam Deary, the head of the...had his office there.

speaker: ES
words: But that was the main office, there was another office that you came into

speaker: ES
words: first. I forget the name of that office or the manager of that office. But

speaker: ES
words: anyway, I presented my petition there, and he looked at it, and he said that

speaker: ES
words: he would take it into the main office. He took it in there and it was turned

speaker: ES
words: down immediately. Mr. Deary said he would have no road built across that,

speaker: ES
words: on account of danger of fires being set by people travelling through. Uell

speaker: ES
words: I got a bright idea, so I told him I should think that road would be an advantage

speaker: ES
words: through there because if any lightning fire or other fires whould get started,

speaker: ES
words: they would have a road to get closer to their timber, to fight the fire. So

speaker: ES
words: the petition went back into Mr. Deary's office and he signed it with reservation.

speaker: ES
words: He said that they should not cut any timber that was not actually needed for

speaker: ES
words: the road, and not build a right of way through there. And we agreed to that,

speaker: ES
words: so the road was built. No trouble.

speaker: ES
words: The road being referred to is "the east route" which had caused some controversy,

speaker: SAM
words: He just turned it down and then he changed his mind and approved it on the

speaker: SAM
words: spot, huh?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, he did. So then the survey was made permanent, and the road was

speaker: ES
words: built. And they complied with that pretty well. They didn't cut trees that

speaker: ES
words: were not needed. See, as the road was surveyed, they just cut timber wide

speaker: ES
words: enough to form a roadway through. And the road was narrow, so that if two

speaker: ES
words: trucks met, why they had to work their way carefully to get by, you know.

speaker: ES
words: But it answered the purpose until they got a better road.
